text: The video demonstrates the extrahepatic approach for a right posterior sectionectomy, focusing on the tumor located in the posterior section of the liver. The liver is visually deep-seated within the rib cage.

Four 8mm trocars are positioned in the upper quadrant of the abdomen for robotic instruments, with trochar number four placed below the left rib cage to prevent collision with trochar number three. An assistant operates a laparoscopic instrument or suction device through a 12mm trochar.
The surgeon uses a long plastic tube to apply the Pringles maneuver through a two-millimeter incision site, temporarily occluding blood flow to the liver.

The surgeon performs a cholecystectomy to expose the hilum of the liver. A four-by-four gauze is used for cushioning or clearing, with one gauze handed to the assistant and another placed in the subhepatic area.

The surgeon identifies the right posterior glycemic particle near the lumbar region and attempts to separate it from the liver parenchyma. The rubirial glucose is widened to facilitate access to the glycemic particle.
The surgeon performs dissection around the glistening pedicle at the hilum of the liver, carefully separating the pedicle from surrounding tissue.

The surgeon coagulates the caudate branch of the polar vein using a bipolar energy device. Dissection behind the particle is challenging, and small branches are carefully divided. Breathing control is maintained with bipolar forceps.

The surgeon performs further dissection beyond the lumbar trachea, widening the space to facilitate isolation of the glistening pedicle. A string is inserted behind the particle and maneuvered to the opposite side.

The surgeon encircles and ligates the pad curve using a string, followed by the application of a plastic clip for secure ligation. The anatomical resection line is marked under the PolarSense camera, indicating the area for resection.

The surgeon performs parenchymal dissection for the right posterior sectionectomy using a Leber parting camera. The dissection follows the resection line with small bites, employing crushing and bipolar coagulation. Fine dissection is achieved with an energy device's active blade vibration. The pathway of the right posterior glycemic particle is identified, and a small section of tissue is resected.

The surgeon divides the previously clipped right posterior glycemic particle. The Pringle maneuver is applied to minimize bleeding, creating a clearer operative field. Intersectional pharyngeal dissection is advanced using a combination of bipolar crushing and blade fine dissection techniques.

The surgeon performs deep panning camera dissection, encountering the right hepatic vein. A small branch of the hepatic vein is isolated and divided. The caudate lobe is transected, exposing the inferior vena cava. A large right inferior hepatic vein is resected after applying plastic clips.

The surgeon completes the liver parenchymal dissection around the right hepatic vein. The specimen is detached from the diaphragm in the bare area and placed into a bin bag. Abdominal pressure is applied while irrigation and hemostasis are performed.
